title: Cuba's Oil Shortage: Supply Collapse, Sanctions Narratives, and What the Tanker Data Actually Shows
narrative: In May 2026, Cuba's state oil company Cupet declared a supply emergency. The Cuban government blamed US sanctions. The US State Department framed it as evidence of sanctions efficacy. Venezuela's PDVSA shipments — the island's primary energy lifeline — were reportedly delayed. The narrative was convenient on all sides. What the physical record actually shows is more complicated:  Venezuelan domestic oil production has been in structural decline for years, independent of any US action; AIS tracking data for PDVSA tankers in the relevant window is sparse and inconsistent with a simple 'sanctions blocked it' story; and the Russian supply line, historically a secondary but consistent contributor, shows no arrivals in open sources for 2026. The oil shortage is real. The cause is contested. The evidence points in multiple directions.
telemetry: Cupet public statement (BBC, May 2026) — declared supply emergency, attributed cause to 'US sanctions' and delayed PDVSA shipments.
AIS tracking data for PDVSA tankers, April-May 2026 — no vessel broadcasting destination Havana in open AIS databases for the reported shipment window.
PDVSA public production reports, Q1-Q2 2026 — Venezuelan crude production remains below 500 kb/d, structurally insufficient to honor all contract commitments.
PDVSA export data, April-May 2026 — no new long-term supply contracts with Cuba filed in open shipping registries.
Russian vessel arrivals at Cuban ports (Marina del Norte, Santiago de Cuba), January-May 2026 — zero confirmed arrivals from Russian-flagged tankers in open AIS / port records.
US Treasury/OFAC sanctions database, April-May 2026 — no new Cuba-specific sanctions actions published in the relevant window.
Cuban power grid data (secondary sources) — multiple reported blackout events in April-May 2026, consistent with genuine supply shortfall.
analysis:
The 'Cuba ran out of oil' headline obscures a supply chain failure that has multiple contributing causes, only one of which is US sanctions.

The Venezuela angle is underreported and structurally determined. PDVSA's domestic production has been in secular decline since the mid-2010s, exacerbated by US sanctions on PDVSA itself since 2019. Venezuela's crude output at sub-500 kb/d is a fraction of its historical peak. When PDVSA cannot simultaneously service debt obligations, domestic demand, and export commitments to allies like Cuba, Cuba is the variable that absorbs the shortfall. This is not a new pattern — it has been documented in Lloyd's Market intelligence and shipping circulars for years. The current shortage is the latest iteration of a structural problem, not a sanctions inflection point.

The AIS data gap is more neutral than either side's narrative implies. PDVSA tankers traveling to Cuba have historically used AIS selectively — turning transponders off near US naval jurisdictions in the Caribbean to avoid tracking. The absence of AIS signatures in open databases does not prove a tanker was not en route; it is consistent with standard operational practice for this supply lane. The absence of confirmed port discharge records at Cuban facilities is a stronger data point — but port authority data for Cuban facilities is sparse in open sources.

The Russian supply line has been a consistent secondary source for Cuba since 2022, but no Russian-flagged vessel arrivals are documented in open AIS or port records for 2026. Whether Russian supply has been redirected, suspended, or is operating below AIS transparency thresholds is not resolvable from open sources. This is a genuine information gap, not evidence of either outcome.

The US framing — 'sanctions work' — conflates two different mechanisms. Primary US sanctions on Venezuelan crude target PDVSA's ability to export to any buyer willing to purchase. Secondary sanctions risk on tankers carrying Venezuelan crude to Cuba adds additional cost. Both mechanisms are real. But a supply shortage driven by PDVSA's internal production collapse is not the same evidence as a supply shortage driven by successful sanctions interdiction. The telemetry does not clearly distinguish between these two mechanisms.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: Cuba's oil shortage is genuine, as evidenced by grid blackout telemetry and Cupet's own public acknowledgment. The 'sanctions did it' narrative is politically convenient but not clearly supported by the physical record. Venezuelan production collapse — structural, years in the making — is a more parsimonious explanation for the delivery failure than active sanctions interdiction. The Russian supply gap is a genuine unknown. Until tanker AIS data or port discharge records corroborate a specific interdiction event, the shortage is a supply collapse story wearing a sanctions narrative costume.
